1:
"And when the people complained, it displeased the LORD: and the LORD heard it; and his anger was kindled; and the fire of the LORD burnt among them, and consumed them that were in the uttermost parts of the camp." The witnesses note the people's murmuring kindling the LORD's anger, the fire breaking out in the camp's outskirts. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were truly complainers, not merely like them, and that what they complained of is not said, it being a thing for which there was no real ground. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read them as those who murmur in the ears of Jehovah of some misfortune, the words expressing general discontent. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the people complained — truly complainers, without real ground; the it displeased the LORD — their discontent provoking him; the his anger kindled — the fire breaking out in the camp; the the uttermost parts — the outskirts consumed.

The witnesses agree the sin was groundless ingratitude: scarcely set out from Sinai, marching toward the promised land under God's own guidance, the people fell to complaining without cause, and their discontent was no light thing but provoked the LORD to send fire; the lesson is sharp — murmuring against God, even when it names no grievance, is heard in heaven and kindles his anger, for it quarrels with his good providence.

So the witnesses make the verse the groundless murmur: The witnesses note the the people complained: truly complainers, without real ground. The witnesses note the it displeased the LORD: their discontent provoking him. The witnesses note the his anger kindled: the fire breaking out in the camp. The witnesses note the the uttermost parts: the outskirts consumed. The expositors observe the the people complained: truly complainers, without real ground. The expositors observe the it displeased the LORD: their discontent provoking him. The expositors observe the his anger kindled: the fire breaking out in the camp. The expositors observe the the uttermost parts: the outskirts consumed. The believer is taught to mark the the people complained, the it displeased the LORD, the his anger kindled, the the uttermost parts, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that the people's groundless murmuring kindled the LORD's anger and fire — discontent quarrelling with his good providence, heard in heaven and judged.
2:
"And the people cried unto Moses; and when Moses prayed unto the LORD, the fire was quenched." The witnesses note the people crying to Moses in terror, and the fire quenched at his prayer. The witnesses note that Gill notes they entreated Moses to pray for them, frightened at the fire consuming many, lest it spread and become general. The witnesses note that the fire was quenched when Moses prayed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the people cried — running to Moses in their terror; the Moses prayed — interceding for the guilty people; the the fire quenched — the judgment stayed at his prayer; the the worth of intercession — the prayer of the mediator prevailing; the the mediator — standing between the people and the fire.

The witnesses agree the mercy came through the mediator's intercession: the same people who had provoked God now ran to Moses to pray for them, and his prayer prevailed to stay the judgment; the scene sets forth the worth of intercession and the office of a mediator who stands between the sinning people and the consuming fire — a shadow of the greater Mediator whose prayer turns away wrath.

So the witnesses make the verse the fire quenched: The witnesses note the the people cried: running to Moses in their terror. The witnesses note the Moses prayed: interceding for the guilty people. The witnesses note the the fire quenched: the judgment stayed at his prayer. The witnesses note the the worth of intercession: the prayer of the mediator prevailing. The witnesses note the the mediator: standing between the people and the fire. The expositors observe the the people cried: running to Moses in their terror. The expositors observe the Moses prayed: interceding for the guilty people. The expositors observe the the fire quenched: the judgment stayed at his prayer. The expositors observe the the worth of intercession: the prayer of the mediator prevailing. The expositors observe the the mediator: standing between the people and the fire. The believer is taught to mark the the people cried, the Moses prayed, the the fire quenched, the the worth of intercession, the the mediator, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that the fire was quenched at Moses' prayer — the worth of intercession, the mediator standing between the sinning people and the consuming fire.
3:
"And he called the name of the place Taberah: because the fire of the LORD burnt among them." The witnesses note the place named Taberah, "burning," in memory of the fire of the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses called it Taberah, that is, "burning." The witnesses note that Wesley notes it had two names — Taberah from this fire, and Kibroth-hattaavah from another occasion — it being no new thing for a place to bear two names, both imposed for memorial. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the called Taberah — that is, "burning."; the the fire of the LORD — the judgment it memorialised; the two names — also Kibroth-hattaavah from another occasion; the a name of judgment — the very ground witnessing against the sin; the a monument of warning — the memory of God's anger preserved.

The witnesses agree the name was set as a lasting witness against the sin: the very ground was called "burning," that Israel might remember what their murmuring had brought; place-names of judgment stood through the wilderness as monuments of warning, the memory of God's anger preserved in a word, that later generations passing by might learn to fear the LORD and not provoke him as their fathers did.

So the witnesses make the verse the name Taberah: The witnesses note the called Taberah: that is, "burning." The witnesses note the the fire of the LORD: the judgment it memorialised. The witnesses note the two names: also Kibroth-hattaavah from another occasion. The witnesses note the a name of judgment: the very ground witnessing against the sin. The witnesses note the a monument of warning: the memory of God's anger preserved. The expositors observe the called Taberah: that is, "burning." The expositors observe the the fire of the LORD: the judgment it memorialised. The expositors observe the two names: also Kibroth-hattaavah from another occasion. The expositors observe the a name of judgment: the very ground witnessing against the sin. The expositors observe the a monument of warning: the memory of God's anger preserved. The believer is taught to mark the called Taberah, the the fire of the LORD, the two names, the a name of judgment, the a monument of warning, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that the place was named Taberah, "burning" — a lasting monument of warning, the memory of God's anger against murmuring preserved in a name.
4:
"And the mixt multitude that was among them fell a lusting: and the children of Israel also wept again, and said, Who shall give us flesh to eat?" The witnesses note the lust beginning with the mixed multitude and spreading to Israel — a craving for flesh. The witnesses note that Gill notes the mixed multitude came out of Egypt with them — not only Egyptians but a mixture of divers people, joined to Israel in hope of sharing their blessings. The witnesses note that their lusting drew Israel also into weeping and craving flesh. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the mixt multitude — the half-hearted who came from Egypt; the fell a lusting — beginning the craving for flesh; the Israel also wept — catching the discontent; the who shall give us flesh — despising present mercy for what is denied.

The witnesses agree the contagion of evil desire spread from the half-hearted to the whole camp: the mixed multitude, never truly God's people, began the lusting, and Israel caught it from them; the lesson warns of the danger of unequal company, the loosely-joined drawing the people of God into discontent, and of the craving that despises present mercy to hanker after what is denied.

So the witnesses make the verse the spreading lust: The witnesses note the the mixt multitude: the half-hearted who came from Egypt. The witnesses note the fell a lusting: beginning the craving for flesh. The witnesses note the Israel also wept: catching the discontent. The witnesses note the who shall give us flesh: despising present mercy for what is denied. The expositors observe the the mixt multitude: the half-hearted who came from Egypt. The expositors observe the fell a lusting: beginning the craving for flesh. The expositors observe the Israel also wept: catching the discontent. The expositors observe the who shall give us flesh: despising present mercy for what is denied. The believer is taught to mark the the mixt multitude, the fell a lusting, the Israel also wept, the who shall give us flesh, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that the lust began with the mixed multitude and spread to Israel — the contagion of evil desire, the loosely-joined drawing God's people into discontent.
5:
"We remember the fish, which we did eat in Egypt freely; the cucumbers, and the melons, and the leeks, and the onions, and the garlick:" The witnesses note the people's fond remembrance of Egypt's food — fish, cucumbers, melons, leeks, onions, and garlic. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Egyptians lived much on fish, the Nile abounding with it cheaply, and on the herbs and roots here named. The witnesses note that they claimed to have eaten the fish "freely," that is, cheaply or without cost. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the fish... freely — remembered as cheap and plentiful; the cucumbers, melons, leeks — the herbs and roots of Egypt; the the bondage forgotten — remembering the food, not the lash; the looking back — the heart unweaned, pining for Egypt.

The witnesses agree the memory was both false and base: they remembered Egypt's cheap fare but forgot its bondage and the lash, magnifying the leeks and onions of slavery while despising the bread of heaven; the craving looked backward to the house of bondage with longing, the surest mark of a heart unweaned from the world — to pine for the pleasures of Egypt is to forget the misery from which God redeemed them.

So the witnesses make the verse the food of Egypt: The witnesses note the the fish... freely: remembered as cheap and plentiful. The witnesses note the cucumbers, melons, leeks: the herbs and roots of Egypt. The witnesses note the the bondage forgotten: remembering the food, not the lash. The witnesses note the looking back: the heart unweaned, pining for Egypt. The expositors observe the the fish... freely: remembered as cheap and plentiful. The expositors observe the cucumbers, melons, leeks: the herbs and roots of Egypt. The expositors observe the the bondage forgotten: remembering the food, not the lash. The expositors observe the looking back: the heart unweaned, pining for Egypt. The believer is taught to mark the the fish... freely, the cucumbers, melons, leeks, the the bondage forgotten, the looking back, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that they remembered Egypt's fish and herbs but forgot its bondage — the craving looking back with longing, the mark of a heart unweaned from the world.
6:
"But now our soul is dried away: there is nothing at all, beside this manna, before our eyes." The witnesses note the people's contempt of the manna — "nothing at all" but this, their souls dried away for want of flesh. The witnesses note that Gill notes they meant their bodies, pretending to be half-starved and wasting for want of flesh food. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads it, "there is nothing, except that our eyes look upon the manna." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the our soul dried away — feigning to waste for want of flesh; the nothing at all — the heavenly bread counted as nothing; the this manna — the daily gift of God loathed; the rank ingratitude — despising the gift and insulting the Giver.

The witnesses agree the complaint was rank ingratitude: the manna was bread from heaven, given daily without toil, yet they called it "nothing at all," loathing the very gift of God; to despise the heavenly bread for the flesh-pots of Egypt is the height of unthankfulness — a warning against the discontent that, surrounded by mercy, can see "nothing" but what it lacks, and so insults the Giver in scorning his gift.

So the witnesses make the verse the manna despised: The witnesses note the our soul dried away: feigning to waste for want of flesh. The witnesses note the nothing at all: the heavenly bread counted as nothing. The witnesses note the this manna: the daily gift of God loathed. The witnesses note the rank ingratitude: despising the gift and insulting the Giver. The expositors observe the our soul dried away: feigning to waste for want of flesh. The expositors observe the nothing at all: the heavenly bread counted as nothing. The expositors observe the this manna: the daily gift of God loathed. The expositors observe the rank ingratitude: despising the gift and insulting the Giver. The believer is taught to mark the our soul dried away, the nothing at all, the this manna, the rank ingratitude, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that the people despised the manna as "nothing at all" — rank ingratitude, the discontent that amid mercy sees nothing but what it lacks and scorns the Giver.
7:
"And the manna was as coriander seed, and the colour thereof as the colour of bdellium." The witnesses note the description of the manna — like coriander seed in form, the colour of bdellium. The witnesses note that Gill notes the manna was white, the likeness to coriander seed being in its round form and small quantity, falling in small round grains. The witnesses note that this is set down to expose the folly and ingratitude of Israel, who despised so good a provision. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the as coriander seed — small, round grains; the the colour of bdellium — fair and pleasant to see; the a good provision — wholesome bread from heaven; the to shame the murmurers — the gift's excellence rebuking their scorn.

The witnesses agree the very description rebukes the murmurers: the manna is portrayed as a fine, fair, and wholesome food, that the reader may see how good was the gift they scorned; the Scripture lingers on its qualities to shame their ingratitude, the bread of heaven set before us in its excellence that we may marvel at a people who could loathe it — and learn not to despise the daily mercies of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the manna described: The witnesses note the as coriander seed: small, round grains. The witnesses note the the colour of bdellium: fair and pleasant to see. The witnesses note the a good provision: wholesome bread from heaven. The witnesses note the to shame the murmurers: the gift's excellence rebuking their scorn. The expositors observe the as coriander seed: small, round grains. The expositors observe the the colour of bdellium: fair and pleasant to see. The expositors observe the a good provision: wholesome bread from heaven. The expositors observe the to shame the murmurers: the gift's excellence rebuking their scorn. The believer is taught to mark the as coriander seed, the the colour of bdellium, the a good provision, the to shame the murmurers, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that the manna was a fair and wholesome food — the description rebuking the murmurers, the excellence of the gift shaming their ingratitude.
8:
"And the people went about, and gathered it, and ground it in mills, or beat it in a mortar... and the taste of it was as the taste of fresh oil." The witnesses note how the manna was gathered and prepared — ground or beaten, baked, its taste as fresh oil. The witnesses note that Gill notes they went about the camp and gathered it every morning, having it for the gathering without any expense. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads the taste as "of a fat cake of oil," compared with the "wafers made with honey" of Exod. 16:31. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the gathered each morning — had for the gathering, without cost; the ground or beaten — prepared in many ways; the the taste of fresh oil — like cakes of oil and honey; the freely and richly given — the complaint the more inexcusable.

The witnesses agree the manna was both freely given and richly satisfying: it cost them nothing but the gathering, and could be prepared in many ways, its taste like the finest cakes of oil and honey; so bountiful was the provision that their complaint is the more inexcusable — the bread of heaven was not a bare and tasteless ration but a generous and pleasant food, freely set at their doors each morning.

So the witnesses make the verse the manna prepared: The witnesses note the gathered each morning: had for the gathering, without cost. The witnesses note the ground or beaten: prepared in many ways. The witnesses note the the taste of fresh oil: like cakes of oil and honey. The witnesses note the freely and richly given: the complaint the more inexcusable. The expositors observe the gathered each morning: had for the gathering, without cost. The expositors observe the ground or beaten: prepared in many ways. The expositors observe the the taste of fresh oil: like cakes of oil and honey. The expositors observe the freely and richly given: the complaint the more inexcusable. The believer is taught to mark the gathered each morning, the ground or beaten, the the taste of fresh oil, the freely and richly given, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that the manna was freely gathered and richly tasting, like cakes of oil — a generous provision that makes the people's complaint the more inexcusable.
9:
"And when the dew fell upon the camp in the night, the manna fell upon it." The witnesses note the manna falling with the night dew upon the camp, a daily and constant gift. The witnesses note that Gill notes the dew fell upon the camp in the night, as it usually and constantly did, and the manna with it. The witnesses note that the provision came silently and faithfully each night. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the dew fell — upon the camp in the night; the the manna with it — the bread coming down with the dew; the while they slept — the food ready without labour; the daily faithfulness — the provision never failing.

The witnesses agree the manner of the gift was a daily wonder of grace: while the people slept, God sent down the dew and the bread upon it, so that every morning they found their food ready without labour; the steady, silent faithfulness of the provision — never failing through all the years of the wilderness — is a standing proof of God's care, that he feeds his people day by day, and a rebuke to the unbelief that could doubt so faithful a Giver.

So the witnesses make the verse the nightly manna: The witnesses note the the dew fell: upon the camp in the night. The witnesses note the the manna with it: the bread coming down with the dew. The witnesses note the while they slept: the food ready without labour. The witnesses note the daily faithfulness: the provision never failing. The expositors observe the the dew fell: upon the camp in the night. The expositors observe the the manna with it: the bread coming down with the dew. The expositors observe the while they slept: the food ready without labour. The expositors observe the daily faithfulness: the provision never failing. The believer is taught to mark the the dew fell, the the manna with it, the while they slept, the daily faithfulness, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that the manna fell with the night dew, ready each morning — the daily, silent faithfulness of God's care, a rebuke to the unbelief that doubted so faithful a Giver.
10:
"Then Moses heard the people weep throughout their families, every man in the door of his tent: and the anger of the LORD was kindled greatly; Moses also was displeased." The witnesses note the people weeping throughout their families, the LORD's anger kindled, and Moses himself displeased and burdened. The witnesses note that Gill notes their lusting was so general and their distress so great that they wept and cried aloud, every family at its tent door. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads Moses displeased at their murmuring and oppressed with the heavy burden of responsibility. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the weep throughout their families — openly, at every tent door; the a general discontent — the contagion through the whole camp; the the LORD's anger kindled — their sin provoking him greatly; the Moses displeased — crushed under the burden.

The witnesses agree the scene is one of widespread and shameless discontent: the weeping was not in secret but openly, family by family at every tent door, a contagion of complaint through the whole camp; it kindled God's anger and crushed Moses' spirit, the leader bowed under the double weight of the people's sin and his own charge — a picture of how the murmuring of a people grieves both God and the faithful servant set over them.

So the witnesses make the verse the weeping camp: The witnesses note the weep throughout their families: openly, at every tent door. The witnesses note the a general discontent: the contagion through the whole camp. The witnesses note the the LORD's anger kindled: their sin provoking him greatly. The witnesses note the Moses displeased: crushed under the burden. The expositors observe the weep throughout their families: openly, at every tent door. The expositors observe the a general discontent: the contagion through the whole camp. The expositors observe the the LORD's anger kindled: their sin provoking him greatly. The expositors observe the Moses displeased: crushed under the burden. The believer is taught to mark the weep throughout their families, the a general discontent, the the LORD's anger kindled, the Moses displeased, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that the people wept openly through the camp, kindling God's anger and crushing Moses — the murmuring of a people grieving both God and his faithful servant.
11:
"And Moses said unto the LORD, Wherefore hast thou afflicted thy servant? and wherefore have I not found favour in thy sight, that thou layest the burden of all this people upon me?" The witnesses note Moses' grieved complaint to the LORD — feeling himself afflicted and overburdened with the people. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses does not seem in a good frame throughout this discourse, even the best of men not always alike, sometimes acting contrary to their own grace. The witnesses note that he felt the government of the people as a crushing weight laid on him. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the wherefore afflicted — feeling burdened rather than honoured; the not found favour — as if out of God's good will; the the burden laid on me — the government a crushing weight; the the weakness of a saint — even the meekest in a dark hour.

The witnesses agree Moses here shows the weakness of even the meekest servant: bowed under the burden, he speaks to God in a peevish and fretful spirit, as though afflicted rather than honoured by his charge; yet the witnesses mark it tenderly, for the best of men have their dark hours, and Moses' very complaint, poured out to God rather than hidden, shows where the burdened soul should carry its trouble — to the LORD himself.

So the witnesses make the verse Moses' complaint: The witnesses note the wherefore afflicted: feeling burdened rather than honoured. The witnesses note the not found favour: as if out of God's good will. The witnesses note the the burden laid on me: the government a crushing weight. The witnesses note the the weakness of a saint: even the meekest in a dark hour. The expositors observe the wherefore afflicted: feeling burdened rather than honoured. The expositors observe the not found favour: as if out of God's good will. The expositors observe the the burden laid on me: the government a crushing weight. The expositors observe the the weakness of a saint: even the meekest in a dark hour. The believer is taught to mark the wherefore afflicted, the not found favour, the the burden laid on me, the the weakness of a saint, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that Moses, bowed under the burden, complained to God in a fretful spirit — the weakness of even the meekest servant, yet his trouble carried to the LORD himself.
12:
"Have I conceived all this people? have I begotten them, that thou shouldest say unto me, Carry them in thy bosom... unto the land which thou swarest unto their fathers?" The witnesses note Moses pleading that he is not the parent of the people, to bear them as a nurse in his bosom. The witnesses note that Gill notes that though Moses was not their parent in a literal sense, yet in a civil and political sense he was their father, as every governor should have a paternal affection and care for his people. The witnesses note that the figure of carrying in the bosom is that of a nursing-father bearing the child. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the have I conceived them — protesting he is not their parent; the carry them in thy bosom — the charge of a nurse bearing a child; the to the promised land — the whole journey laid on him; the God the true bearer — the LORD bearing his people as a father (Deut. 1:31).

The witnesses agree Moses recoils from a burden he feels too great for him: he protests that he did not give the people being, that he should be charged to carry them as a nurse carries a sucking child all the way to Canaan; yet the very image he rejects is the one God himself owns toward his people, the LORD bearing them as a father bears his son (cf. Deut. 1:31) — the weight Moses found unbearable resting in truth on God's own everlasting arms.

So the witnesses make the verse the nursing-father's burden: The witnesses note the have I conceived them: protesting he is not their parent. The witnesses note the carry them in thy bosom: the charge of a nurse bearing a child. The witnesses note the to the promised land: the whole journey laid on him. The witnesses note the God the true bearer: the LORD bearing his people as a father (Deut. 1:31). The expositors observe the have I conceived them: protesting he is not their parent. The expositors observe the carry them in thy bosom: the charge of a nurse bearing a child. The expositors observe the to the promised land: the whole journey laid on him. The expositors observe the God the true bearer: the LORD bearing his people as a father (Deut. 1:31). The believer is taught to mark the have I conceived them, the carry them in thy bosom, the to the promised land, the God the true bearer, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that Moses protested he was not the people's parent to carry them as a nurse — the burden too great for him resting in truth on God, who bears his people as a father (Deut. 1:31).
13:
"Whence should I have flesh to give unto all this people? for they weep unto me, saying, Give us flesh, that we may eat." The witnesses note Moses at a loss where to find flesh for so vast a people who wept to him for it. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses' words seem almost to countenance the people, as if their craving were no evil, though it was out of his power to satisfy them. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes Moses does not justify their murmuring, yet displays a spirit of discontent and almost despair at God's dealings with himself. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the whence should I have flesh — moses at a loss to feed them; the they weep unto me — the multitude crying for flesh; the half-excusing them — his words almost countenancing the craving; the forgetting God's power — insufficiency passing toward unbelief.

The witnesses agree Moses' difficulty was real but his spirit faltering: he rightly saw that he could not himself feed a multitude with flesh, yet in saying it he half-excused the people and sank toward despair, forgetting that the God who fed them with manna could as easily give flesh; the lesson is that even a true sense of our own insufficiency may pass into unbelief if we measure the need by our own resources and forget the power of God.

So the witnesses make the verse whence the flesh: The witnesses note the whence should I have flesh: Moses at a loss to feed them. The witnesses note the they weep unto me: the multitude crying for flesh. The witnesses note the half-excusing them: his words almost countenancing the craving. The witnesses note the forgetting God's power: insufficiency passing toward unbelief. The expositors observe the whence should I have flesh: Moses at a loss to feed them. The expositors observe the they weep unto me: the multitude crying for flesh. The expositors observe the half-excusing them: his words almost countenancing the craving. The expositors observe the forgetting God's power: insufficiency passing toward unbelief. The believer is taught to mark the whence should I have flesh, the they weep unto me, the half-excusing them, the forgetting God's power, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that Moses, at a loss to find flesh, sank toward despair — a true sense of insufficiency passing into unbelief when the need is measured by our own resources, forgetting God's power.
14:
"I am not able to bear all this people alone, because it is too heavy for me." The witnesses note Moses' confession — he cannot bear the people alone, the burden too heavy. The witnesses note that Gill notes that, in truth, Moses was not alone, for the LORD himself was with him in every matter of moment, and had promised and did supply the people. The witnesses note that the burden of government and provision pressed him sorely. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not able alone — the burden beyond his own strength; the too heavy for me — the weight of government and supply; the yet not alone — god with him in every matter; the feeling forsaken — the burdened soul forgetting God's presence.

The witnesses agree the confession was both true and mistaken: it was true that Moses could not bear the people by his own strength, but mistaken to say he was "alone," for God was with him at every step, supplying what he could not; the half-truth shows how a burdened soul, looking only at itself, may feel forsaken even while the LORD stands by — the remedy being to remember that he who lays the burden bears it with us.

So the witnesses make the verse too heavy to bear: The witnesses note the not able alone: the burden beyond his own strength. The witnesses note the too heavy for me: the weight of government and supply. The witnesses note the yet not alone: God with him in every matter. The witnesses note the feeling forsaken: the burdened soul forgetting God's presence. The expositors observe the not able alone: the burden beyond his own strength. The expositors observe the too heavy for me: the weight of government and supply. The expositors observe the yet not alone: God with him in every matter. The expositors observe the feeling forsaken: the burdened soul forgetting God's presence. The believer is taught to mark the not able alone, the too heavy for me, the yet not alone, the feeling forsaken, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that Moses confessed the burden too heavy to bear alone — true of his own strength, but mistaken in "alone," for God was with him, bearing the burden he laid on.
15:
"And if thou deal thus with me, kill me, I pray thee, out of hand, if I have found favour in thy sight; and let me not see my wretchedness." The witnesses note Moses' extremity — begging God to take his life rather than bear the burden longer. The witnesses note that Gill notes he means, let the whole weight of government lie on him and leave him alone to bear it; Ellicott renders the plea, "make an utter end of me." The witnesses note that Wesley reads "my wretchedness" as his torment arising from the difficulty of his office and the dread of the people's ruin and the dishonour to God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the kill me, I pray thee — weary of life under the burden; the let me not see my wretchedness — dreading the people's ruin; the the dishonour to God — grief for God's honour, not mere self-pity; the the language of despair — the servant overwhelmed, needing relief.

The witnesses agree Moses here sinks to his lowest, weary of life under the burden: the dread of the people's destruction and the reproach it would bring on God and religion so crushed him that he begged to die; Wesley finds in his words not mere self-pity but a true grief for God's honour — yet the request is the language of despair, the faithful servant overwhelmed, needing not death but the help and relief that God was about to give.

So the witnesses make the verse the plea for death: The witnesses note the kill me, I pray thee: weary of life under the burden. The witnesses note the let me not see my wretchedness: dreading the people's ruin. The witnesses note the the dishonour to God: grief for God's honour, not mere self-pity. The witnesses note the the language of despair: the servant overwhelmed, needing relief. The expositors observe the kill me, I pray thee: weary of life under the burden. The expositors observe the let me not see my wretchedness: dreading the people's ruin. The expositors observe the the dishonour to God: grief for God's honour, not mere self-pity. The expositors observe the the language of despair: the servant overwhelmed, needing relief. The believer is taught to mark the kill me, I pray thee, the let me not see my wretchedness, the the dishonour to God, the the language of despair, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that Moses begged to die rather than bear the burden — the language of despair, mixed with grief for God's honour, the servant overwhelmed and needing the relief God was about to give.
16:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Gather unto me seventy men of the elders of Israel, whom thou knowest to be the elders of the people, and officers over them; and bring them unto the tabernacle of the congregation, that they may stand there with thee." The witnesses note God's gracious answer — to gather seventy elders to share the burden with Moses. The witnesses note that Gill notes the LORD answered without upbraiding Moses for his unbecoming speeches, but in a kind and tender manner provided for his ease. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note there was good ground for his complaint, the burden of the office being indeed heavy. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the gather seventy men — the elders to share the burden; the to stand with thee — helpers given to Moses; the a real burden — god owning the weight of the office; the mercy, not rebuke — god dealing gently with his servant.

The witnesses agree God met Moses' weakness with mercy, not rebuke: He owned that the burden was real and provided helpers, seventy elders to stand with Moses and share the charge; the tenderness of the answer — meeting a fretful complaint with gracious relief — shows how God deals gently with his overwhelmed servants, not casting them off for their frailty but easing the load and supplying their lack.

So the witnesses make the verse the seventy elders: The witnesses note the gather seventy men: the elders to share the burden. The witnesses note the to stand with thee: helpers given to Moses. The witnesses note the a real burden: God owning the weight of the office. The witnesses note the mercy, not rebuke: God dealing gently with his servant. The expositors observe the gather seventy men: the elders to share the burden. The expositors observe the to stand with thee: helpers given to Moses. The expositors observe the a real burden: God owning the weight of the office. The expositors observe the mercy, not rebuke: God dealing gently with his servant. The believer is taught to mark the gather seventy men, the to stand with thee, the a real burden, the mercy, not rebuke, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that God answered Moses' complaint by giving seventy elders to share the burden — mercy not rebuke, God dealing gently with his overwhelmed servant and easing his load.
17:
"And I will come down and talk with thee there: and I will take of the spirit which is upon thee, and will put it upon them; and they shall bear the burden of the people with thee, that thou bear it not thyself alone." The witnesses note God promising to put his Spirit, which was on Moses, upon the seventy, that they might share the burden. The witnesses note that Gill notes God would come down and talk with Moses face to face, an instance of great condescension and grace, even after Moses' froward spirit, overlooking all. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read the LORD taking of the Spirit upon Moses and putting it on the elders. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the I will come down — god talking with Moses in grace; the take of the spirit upon thee — the same Spirit given to the elders; the bear the burden with thee — the help spiritual, not merely office; the not diminished — the Spirit shared as one flame lights many (Acts 2:17).

The witnesses agree the help God gave was spiritual, not merely administrative: the elders could not bear the burden by office alone but needed the same Spirit that rested on Moses, and God put it upon them; the sharing of the Spirit without its being diminished in Moses — as one flame lights many — foreshadows the day when God would pour his Spirit on all his people (cf. Acts 2:17), the gift multiplied without being lessened.

So the witnesses make the verse the shared Spirit: The witnesses note the I will come down: God talking with Moses in grace. The witnesses note the take of the spirit upon thee: the same Spirit given to the elders. The witnesses note the bear the burden with thee: the help spiritual, not merely office. The witnesses note the not diminished: the Spirit shared as one flame lights many (Acts 2:17). The expositors observe the I will come down: God talking with Moses in grace. The expositors observe the take of the spirit upon thee: the same Spirit given to the elders. The expositors observe the bear the burden with thee: the help spiritual, not merely office. The expositors observe the not diminished: the Spirit shared as one flame lights many (Acts 2:17). The believer is taught to mark the I will come down, the take of the spirit upon thee, the bear the burden with thee, the not diminished, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that God put of the Spirit on Moses upon the seventy, that they bear the burden with him — the gift shared without being lessened, foreshadowing the Spirit poured on all (Acts 2:17).
18:
"And say thou unto the people, Sanctify yourselves against to morrow, and ye shall eat flesh... therefore the LORD will give you flesh, and ye shall eat." The witnesses note the people bidden to sanctify themselves, for the LORD would give them flesh — yet a request granted in judgment. The witnesses note that Gill notes what follows respects the people, as what went before regarded Moses. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads them required to sanctify themselves for the manifestation of the divine presence, though their request was sinful and was granted in judgment as well as mercy (cf. Ps. 106:15, "he gave them their request, but sent leanness into their soul"). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sanctify yourselves — prepared for the divine manifestation; the ye shall eat flesh — the craving to be granted; the a sinful request — their lust despising God's provision; the granted in judgment — the answered lust a curse (Ps. 106:15).

The witnesses agree the granting of the craving was no simple kindness but a judgment: God would indeed give them flesh, in abundance, yet because they had despised his provision and lusted, the gift would prove a curse; Ellicott presses the warning of Psalm 106:15 — to have one's sinful desire granted is among the heaviest judgments, the answered lust bringing leanness to the soul.

So the witnesses make the verse the request granted in judgment: The witnesses note the sanctify yourselves: prepared for the divine manifestation. The witnesses note the ye shall eat flesh: the craving to be granted. The witnesses note the a sinful request: their lust despising God's provision. The witnesses note the granted in judgment: the answered lust a curse (Ps. 106:15). The expositors observe the sanctify yourselves: prepared for the divine manifestation. The expositors observe the ye shall eat flesh: the craving to be granted. The expositors observe the a sinful request: their lust despising God's provision. The expositors observe the granted in judgment: the answered lust a curse (Ps. 106:15). The believer is taught to mark the sanctify yourselves, the ye shall eat flesh, the a sinful request, the granted in judgment, and to be content with God's provision. The verse thus records that God would give them flesh, yet in judgment — the answered lust a curse, the heaviest judgment to have one's sinful desire granted (Ps. 106:15).
19:
"Ye shall not eat one day, nor two days, nor five days, neither ten days, nor twenty days;" The witnesses note God's word that the flesh would be given not for a day or a few, but on and on. The witnesses note that Gill notes they should not eat one day only. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch join this with the following — not one, two, five, ten, or twenty days, but a whole month. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not one day — nor two, nor five, nor ten; the nor twenty days — the count rising toward a month; the a glut, not a meal — the flesh given on and on; the abundance as judgment — the lust over-satisfied to loathing.

The witnesses agree the very abundance was made part of the judgment: God would not give the flesh sparingly but in a glut, day after day, until the craving turned to loathing; the rising count — one, two, five, ten, twenty days — builds toward the dreadful "whole month," showing that to have one's lust over-satisfied is itself a punishment, the desire gratified to the point of disgust.

So the witnesses make the verse the flesh prolonged: The witnesses note the not one day: nor two, nor five, nor ten. The witnesses note the nor twenty days: the count rising toward a month. The witnesses note the a glut, not a meal: the flesh given on and on. The witnesses note the abundance as judgment: the lust over-satisfied to loathing. The expositors observe the not one day: nor two, nor five, nor ten. The expositors observe the nor twenty days: the count rising toward a month. The expositors observe the a glut, not a meal: the flesh given on and on. The expositors observe the abundance as judgment: the lust over-satisfied to loathing. The believer is taught to mark the not one day, the nor twenty days, the a glut, not a meal, the abundance as judgment, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that the flesh would be given day after day, not for a day or a few — the very abundance a judgment, the lust over-satisfied to the point of loathing.
20:
"But even a whole month, until it come out at your nostrils, and it be loathsome unto you: because that ye have despised the LORD which is among you..." The witnesses note the flesh given a whole month, till it grew loathsome — the punishment of despising the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes they continued at Kibroth-hattaavah a whole month, as the Jews conclude from this clause. The witnesses note that Wesley marks that God destroys them by granting their desires, turning their blessings into curses, because they had despised his bounty and distrusted his providence. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a whole month — the flesh given till it sickened them; the out at your nostrils — the craving turned to disgust; the despised the LORD — their sin in slighting his bounty; the blessings turned to curses — god punishing by granting their desire.

The Wesleyan reader (Wesley) draws the solemn lesson: God here punishes by giving, turning the very thing they craved into a curse; because they had lightly esteemed his manifold blessings and distrusted his promises, he gave them their request but made it loathsome — a warning that an answered lust is no mercy, and that to despise the LORD who is among us is to court a judgment that comes wrapped in the gift we sought.

So the witnesses make the verse the loathsome gift: The witnesses note the a whole month: the flesh given till it sickened them. The witnesses note the out at your nostrils: the craving turned to disgust. The witnesses note the despised the LORD: their sin in slighting his bounty. The witnesses note the blessings turned to curses: God punishing by granting their desire. The expositors observe the a whole month: the flesh given till it sickened them. The expositors observe the out at your nostrils: the craving turned to disgust. The expositors observe the despised the LORD: their sin in slighting his bounty. The expositors observe the blessings turned to curses: God punishing by granting their desire. The believer is taught to mark the a whole month, the out at your nostrils, the despised the LORD, the blessings turned to curses, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that the flesh was given a whole month till it grew loathsome — God punishing by granting the desire, the answered lust a curse on those who despised him.
21:
"And Moses said, The people, among whom I am, are six hundred thousand footmen; and thou hast said, I will give them flesh, that they may eat a whole month." The witnesses note Moses objecting from the people's vast number — six hundred thousand on foot — distrusting the promise. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses said this by way of objection, distrusting God's power to perform. The witnesses note that Calvin marks that though Moses' object was right, he fell into unbelief and stumbled at the very threshold, his pious solicitude turning to doubt. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the six hundred thousand — the vast number of the people; the a whole month — flesh promised for so great a host; the distrusting the promise — measuring it by nature's bounds; the a pious doubt — good concern falling into unbelief.

The reformed reader (Calvin) handles Moses tenderly yet plainly: his concern was pious — he feared God's name might be exposed to derision if the promise failed — yet his fear betrayed him into unbelief, measuring the promise of God by the bounds of nature; even a good man, reasoning from the visible to the impossible, may stumble at the threshold of faith, forgetting that with God the number of the people is no obstacle at all.

So the witnesses make the verse Moses' objection: The witnesses note the six hundred thousand: the vast number of the people. The witnesses note the a whole month: flesh promised for so great a host. The witnesses note the distrusting the promise: measuring it by nature's bounds. The witnesses note the a pious doubt: good concern falling into unbelief. The expositors observe the six hundred thousand: the vast number of the people. The expositors observe the a whole month: flesh promised for so great a host. The expositors observe the distrusting the promise: measuring it by nature's bounds. The expositors observe the a pious doubt: good concern falling into unbelief. The believer is taught to mark the six hundred thousand, the a whole month, the distrusting the promise, the a pious doubt, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that Moses objected from the people's vast number — a pious concern fallen into unbelief, measuring God's promise by the bounds of nature.
22:
"Shall the flocks and the herds be slain for them, to suffice them? or shall all the fish of the sea be gathered together for them, to suffice them?" The witnesses note Moses pressing his doubt — could even all their flocks or all the fish of the sea feed so many? The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses suggests that all their cattle would not suffice a month, and that to slay them all would be unwise, leaving none for sacrifice or for breeding in Canaan. The witnesses note that the question reckons only the natural means at hand. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the flocks and herds — too few to feed the host a month; the all the fish of the sea — even these reckoned insufficient; the the natural means — the whole stock of nature canvassed; the looking to the creature — forgetting the God who rains bread.

The witnesses agree Moses' reasoning, though shrewd, betrays the smallness of his faith: he canvasses the flocks and the fish, the whole stock of nature, and finds them too little — which was true, had God been limited to such means; but the very framing of the question shows he had forgotten the God who rained bread from heaven and could as easily rain flesh, the doubt arising from looking to the creature instead of the Creator.

So the witnesses make the verse the flocks and the fish: The witnesses note the the flocks and herds: too few to feed the host a month. The witnesses note the all the fish of the sea: even these reckoned insufficient. The witnesses note the the natural means: the whole stock of nature canvassed. The witnesses note the looking to the creature: forgetting the God who rains bread. The expositors observe the the flocks and herds: too few to feed the host a month. The expositors observe the all the fish of the sea: even these reckoned insufficient. The expositors observe the the natural means: the whole stock of nature canvassed. The expositors observe the looking to the creature: forgetting the God who rains bread. The believer is taught to mark the the flocks and herds, the all the fish of the sea, the the natural means, the looking to the creature, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that Moses asked whether flocks or fish could feed so many — his doubt looking to the creature and forgetting the Creator who rained bread from heaven.
23:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Is the LORD's hand waxed short? thou shalt see now whether my word shall come to pass unto thee or not." The witnesses note God's gentle yet searching rebuke — "Is the LORD's hand waxed short?" The witnesses note that Gill notes the LORD answered Moses' objection without upbraiding him with his sin of unbelief. The witnesses note that Clarke reads it: "Hast thou forgotten the miracles I have already performed? The power that is unlimited can never be diminished." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the waxed short — the unbelief that supposes God's power lessened; the unlimited power — the hand that rained manna not shortened; the thou shalt see — bidden to watch the promise fulfilled; the faith challenged — doubt answered not with anger but a test.

The Wesleyan reader (Clarke) draws out the force: the question rebukes the unbelief that supposes God's power lessened — the hand that divided the sea and rained manna is not shortened, for unlimited power can never be diminished; God answers doubt not with anger but with a challenge to faith, bidding Moses watch and see whether his word would stand, the proof of the promise being its certain fulfilment (cf. Isa. 59:1).

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD's hand: The witnesses note the waxed short: the unbelief that supposes God's power lessened. The witnesses note the unlimited power: the hand that rained manna not shortened. The witnesses note the thou shalt see: bidden to watch the promise fulfilled. The witnesses note the faith challenged: doubt answered not with anger but a test. The expositors observe the waxed short: the unbelief that supposes God's power lessened. The expositors observe the unlimited power: the hand that rained manna not shortened. The expositors observe the thou shalt see: bidden to watch the promise fulfilled. The expositors observe the faith challenged: doubt answered not with anger but a test. The believer is taught to mark the waxed short, the unlimited power, the thou shalt see, the faith challenged, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that God rebuked the doubt with "Is the LORD's hand waxed short?" — unlimited power never diminished, faith challenged to watch the promise surely fulfilled (Isa. 59:1).
24:
"And Moses went out, and told the people the words of the LORD, and gathered the seventy men of the elders of the people, and set them round about the tabernacle." The witnesses note Moses carrying out God's word — gathering the seventy elders about the tabernacle. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses went out from the tabernacle, the house of the Shechinah, where he had been conversing with God. The witnesses note that Henry marks here the performance of God's word to Moses, that he should have help in the government. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses went out — from the presence of God; the told the people — the words of the LORD; the gathered the seventy — the elders set about the tabernacle; the the promised help — provided in the presence of God.

The puritan expositor (Henry) notes the faithful execution: Moses, his own complaint heard and answered, went straightway to do as God commanded, gathering the seventy and setting them about the tabernacle to receive their charge; the help God promised was now provided, the burdened leader obeying the remedy God gave, and the place chosen — round the tabernacle — fitting, that the gift of the Spirit be given in the presence of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the seventy gathered: The witnesses note the Moses went out: from the presence of God. The witnesses note the told the people: the words of the LORD. The witnesses note the gathered the seventy: the elders set about the tabernacle. The witnesses note the the promised help: provided in the presence of God. The expositors observe the Moses went out: from the presence of God. The expositors observe the told the people: the words of the LORD. The expositors observe the gathered the seventy: the elders set about the tabernacle. The expositors observe the the promised help: provided in the presence of God. The believer is taught to mark the Moses went out, the told the people, the gathered the seventy, the the promised help, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that Moses gathered the seventy about the tabernacle as God commanded — the promised help provided, the gift to be given in the presence of God.
25:
"And the LORD came down in a cloud, and spake unto him, and took of the spirit that was upon him, and gave it unto the seventy elders: and... when the spirit rested upon them, they prophesied, and did not cease." The witnesses note the LORD coming down, putting his Spirit on the seventy, and their prophesying. The witnesses note that Gill notes the LORD came down in a cloud of glory, a visible token of his presence. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read that when the Spirit rested on them they prophesied. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD came down — in a cloud of glory; the took of the spirit — the same that was on Moses given to them; the they prophesied — the open sign of the gift received; the the seal of their commission — their authority owned of God.

The witnesses agree the gift was visibly owned by God and outwardly confirmed: the LORD came down in the cloud and gave of Moses' Spirit to the seventy, who at once prophesied as the open sign that they had truly received it; the prophesying — a thing they did then but not afterward — was the seal of their commission, that all the people might know the elders bore the same Spirit as Moses, their authority from God and not from man.

So the witnesses make the verse the Spirit on the seventy: The witnesses note the the LORD came down: in a cloud of glory. The witnesses note the took of the spirit: the same that was on Moses given to them. The witnesses note the they prophesied: the open sign of the gift received. The witnesses note the the seal of their commission: their authority owned of God. The expositors observe the the LORD came down: in a cloud of glory. The expositors observe the took of the spirit: the same that was on Moses given to them. The expositors observe the they prophesied: the open sign of the gift received. The expositors observe the the seal of their commission: their authority owned of God. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD came down, the took of the spirit, the they prophesied, the the seal of their commission, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that the LORD put his Spirit on the seventy and they prophesied — the open seal of their commission, that all might know they bore the same Spirit as Moses.
26:
"But there remained two of the men in the camp, the name of the one was Eldad, and the name of the other Medad... and the spirit rested upon them... and they prophesied in the camp." The witnesses note Eldad and Medad, two of the seventy, prophesying in the camp though they had not gone out to the tabernacle. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were of the seventy summoned, but came not out to the tabernacle when the rest did. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the Spirit rested on them in the camp, to prove to the whole congregation that the gift was God's, not bound to the place. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the remained in the camp — two who came not to the tabernacle; the the spirit rested on them — the gift given where they were; the prophesied in the camp — the same sign apart from the place; the the Spirit's freedom — the gift from God, not bound to a place.

The witnesses agree the case of Eldad and Medad shows the freedom of the Spirit: though they had not come to the appointed place, the Spirit rested on them where they were and they prophesied in the camp, proving that the gift came from God and was not tied to the tabernacle nor to Moses' presence; the Spirit blows where he will, and may rest on the absent and the unexpected as truly as on those gathered at the sacred place.

So the witnesses make the verse Eldad and Medad: The witnesses note the remained in the camp: two who came not to the tabernacle. The witnesses note the the spirit rested on them: the gift given where they were. The witnesses note the prophesied in the camp: the same sign apart from the place. The witnesses note the the Spirit's freedom: the gift from God, not bound to a place. The expositors observe the remained in the camp: two who came not to the tabernacle. The expositors observe the the spirit rested on them: the gift given where they were. The expositors observe the prophesied in the camp: the same sign apart from the place. The expositors observe the the Spirit's freedom: the gift from God, not bound to a place. The believer is taught to mark the remained in the camp, the the spirit rested on them, the prophesied in the camp, the the Spirit's freedom, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that Eldad and Medad prophesied in the camp, apart from the tabernacle — the freedom of the Spirit, the gift from God and not tied to place or presence.
27:
"And there ran a young man, and told Moses, and said, Eldad and Medad do prophesy in the camp." The witnesses note a young man running to report that Eldad and Medad prophesied in the camp. The witnesses note that Gill notes he had heard them prophesy and thought it not right, being done without Moses' knowledge and in a private place, not among the elders. The witnesses note that, whoever the young man was, it was likely with a good design, consulting the glory of God and the honour of Moses. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the ran to Moses — hastening to tell of the prophesying; the thought it not right — done apart from Moses and the elders; the a good design — jealous for God's glory and Moses' honour; the mistaken zeal — taking the Spirit's work for disorder.

The witnesses agree the report sprang from a well-meant but narrow zeal: the young man, jealous for Moses' authority and the order of things, saw the prophesying in the camp as an irregularity and hastened to tell; his motive was good — concern for God's glory and Moses' honour — yet his judgment was mistaken, taking for disorder what was in truth the free working of the Spirit, a caution that zeal without understanding may oppose the very work of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the young man's report: The witnesses note the ran to Moses: hastening to tell of the prophesying. The witnesses note the thought it not right: done apart from Moses and the elders. The witnesses note the a good design: jealous for God's glory and Moses' honour. The witnesses note the mistaken zeal: taking the Spirit's work for disorder. The expositors observe the ran to Moses: hastening to tell of the prophesying. The expositors observe the thought it not right: done apart from Moses and the elders. The expositors observe the a good design: jealous for God's glory and Moses' honour. The expositors observe the mistaken zeal: taking the Spirit's work for disorder. The believer is taught to mark the ran to Moses, the thought it not right, the a good design, the mistaken zeal, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that a young man ran to report Eldad and Medad — a well-meant but narrow zeal, taking for disorder what was the free working of the Spirit.
28:
"And Joshua the son of Nun, the servant of Moses, one of his young men, answered and said, My lord Moses, forbid them." The witnesses note Joshua, Moses' servant, urging that Eldad and Medad be forbidden to prophesy. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua waited on Moses and ministered to him, and was to be his successor. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read Joshua, one of his young men, saying, "My lord Moses, forbid them." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the servant of Moses — loyal, and to be his successor; the forbid them — urging the two be silenced; the out of loyalty — unable to bear a seeming rival to Moses; the misdirected devotion — zeal opposing the Spirit's freedom.

The witnesses agree Joshua's word came of loyalty, not malice: he loved his master and could not bear that any should seem to rival or act independently of him, and so urged that the two be silenced; yet his zeal, like the young man's, was misdirected — the very devotion that made him a faithful servant here moved him to oppose the Spirit's freedom, a reminder that affection for God's ministers may become a jealousy that hinders the wider work of God.

So the witnesses make the verse Joshua's zeal: The witnesses note the the servant of Moses: loyal, and to be his successor. The witnesses note the forbid them: urging the two be silenced. The witnesses note the out of loyalty: unable to bear a seeming rival to Moses. The witnesses note the misdirected devotion: zeal opposing the Spirit's freedom. The expositors observe the the servant of Moses: loyal, and to be his successor. The expositors observe the forbid them: urging the two be silenced. The expositors observe the out of loyalty: unable to bear a seeming rival to Moses. The expositors observe the misdirected devotion: zeal opposing the Spirit's freedom. The believer is taught to mark the the servant of Moses, the forbid them, the out of loyalty, the misdirected devotion, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that Joshua urged the two be forbidden — a loyalty to Moses turned, like the young man's zeal, to a jealousy that would hinder the wider work of God.
29:
"And Moses said unto him, Enviest thou for my sake? would God that all the LORD'S people were prophets, and that the LORD would put his spirit upon them!" The witnesses note Moses' noble answer — far from envy, he wished all the LORD's people were prophets. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses asks whether Joshua envied for his sake, lest his authority be lessened because the two prophesied apart from him. The witnesses note that Clarke turns it to verse — Moses rebuking our partial love, who envy the gifts bestowed on those we disapprove, not knowing our own spirit. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the enviest thou for my sake — disowning all jealousy for his honour; the would God all were prophets — longing the gift be shared by all; the the Spirit on them — desiring the Spirit poured on the people; the answered at Pentecost — the wish fulfilled when the Spirit came on all (Acts 2:17).

The witnesses agree Moses here shines in true greatness of soul: so far from grudging the gift to others or guarding his own pre-eminence, he longed that the whole people might share the Spirit and prophesy; the magnanimous wish — "would God all the LORD's people were prophets" — looks beyond the law's few to the gospel's many, and was answered at last when the Spirit was poured on all flesh at Pentecost (cf. Acts 2:17; Joel 2:28).

So the witnesses make the verse Moses' magnanimity: The witnesses note the enviest thou for my sake: disowning all jealousy for his honour. The witnesses note the would God all were prophets: longing the gift be shared by all. The witnesses note the the Spirit on them: desiring the Spirit poured on the people. The witnesses note the answered at Pentecost: the wish fulfilled when the Spirit came on all (Acts 2:17). The expositors observe the enviest thou for my sake: disowning all jealousy for his honour. The expositors observe the would God all were prophets: longing the gift be shared by all. The expositors observe the the Spirit on them: desiring the Spirit poured on the people. The expositors observe the answered at Pentecost: the wish fulfilled when the Spirit came on all (Acts 2:17). The believer is taught to mark the enviest thou for my sake, the would God all were prophets, the the Spirit on them, the answered at Pentecost, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that Moses, far from envy, wished all the LORD's people were prophets — a magnanimity looking beyond the few to the many, answered when the Spirit was poured on all flesh (Joel 2:28; Acts 2:17).
30:
"And Moses gat him into the camp, he and the elders of Israel." The witnesses note Moses returning into the camp with the elders, the matter settled. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses went from the tabernacle door, where he had settled the elders, into the camp — perhaps to look into the affair of Eldad and Medad and put them among the elders, or to do public business with their help. The witnesses note that he now had the help God had promised. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses gat him in — returning to the government; the he and the elders — his new helpers with him; the the dispute answered — eldad and Medad owned, not silenced; the Moses' hands strengthened — the work enlarged, not diminished.

The witnesses agree the verse marks the quiet resumption of order: the gift given and the dispute answered, Moses went back into the camp with his new helpers to take up the government again; the seventy were now joined to him in the work, and Eldad and Medad, far from being silenced, were owned among them — the whole episode ending in the strengthening of Moses' hands and the enlargement, not the diminishing, of the Spirit's work.

So the witnesses make the verse the return to the camp: The witnesses note the Moses gat him in: returning to the government. The witnesses note the he and the elders: his new helpers with him. The witnesses note the the dispute answered: Eldad and Medad owned, not silenced. The witnesses note the Moses' hands strengthened: the work enlarged, not diminished. The expositors observe the Moses gat him in: returning to the government. The expositors observe the he and the elders: his new helpers with him. The expositors observe the the dispute answered: Eldad and Medad owned, not silenced. The expositors observe the Moses' hands strengthened: the work enlarged, not diminished. The believer is taught to mark the Moses gat him in, the he and the elders, the the dispute answered, the Moses' hands strengthened, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that Moses returned into the camp with the elders — his hands strengthened by the gift, the Spirit's work enlarged, not diminished, by the day's events.
31:
"And there went forth a wind from the LORD, and brought quails from the sea, and let them fall by the camp... and as it were two cubits high upon the face of the earth." The witnesses note the wind from the LORD bringing the quails, letting them fall about the camp in vast abundance. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was no common wind but one immediately raised by the Lord, an east and a south wind, to bring the quails to the camp. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch describe the quails let fall about the camp, the abundance answering the promise. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a wind from the LORD — no common wind, but his raising; the quails from the sea — driven to the camp by his power; the let them fall by the camp — in vast and heaped abundance; the the doubt rebuked — the impossible done at a word (v.22).

The witnesses agree the giving of the flesh was as plainly God's work as the manna: a wind of his raising drove the quails from the sea and dropped them in heaps about the camp, the very abundance proving that the LORD's hand was not shortened; what Moses thought impossible was done at a word, the flesh given in such plenty that the doubt of v.22 was rebuked by the heaps lying two cubits deep.

So the witnesses make the verse the quails brought: The witnesses note the a wind from the LORD: no common wind, but his raising. The witnesses note the quails from the sea: driven to the camp by his power. The witnesses note the let them fall by the camp: in vast and heaped abundance. The witnesses note the the doubt rebuked: the impossible done at a word (v.22). The expositors observe the a wind from the LORD: no common wind, but his raising. The expositors observe the quails from the sea: driven to the camp by his power. The expositors observe the let them fall by the camp: in vast and heaped abundance. The expositors observe the the doubt rebuked: the impossible done at a word (v.22). The believer is taught to mark the a wind from the LORD, the quails from the sea, the let them fall by the camp, the the doubt rebuked, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that a wind of the LORD brought the quails in heaps about the camp — the impossible done at a word, Moses' doubt rebuked by the abundance, the LORD's hand not shortened.
32:
"And the people stood up all that day, and all that night, and all the next day, and they gathered the quails: he that gathered least gathered ten homers..." The witnesses note the people gathering the quails greedily, day and night, the least gathering ten homers. The witnesses note that Gill notes they stood up all that day on which the quails fell in the morning, gathering without rest. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note they gathered all that day and night and the next, spreading them about the camp. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all day and night — gathering without rest; the ten homers the least — a vast heap for every man; the spread about the camp — the flesh laid out in plenty; the insatiable lust — grasping to excess what they craved.

The witnesses agree the greedy gathering betrayed the very lust that brought the judgment: the people gave themselves wholly to heaping up the flesh, working a day and a night and a day without pause, the least securing ten homers; their insatiable grasping after what they had craved shows the heart of the sin — not hunger, for they had bread, but a lust that could not be satisfied, gathering to excess what would soon be their bane.

So the witnesses make the verse the greedy gathering: The witnesses note the all day and night: gathering without rest. The witnesses note the ten homers the least: a vast heap for every man. The witnesses note the spread about the camp: the flesh laid out in plenty. The witnesses note the insatiable lust: grasping to excess what they craved. The expositors observe the all day and night: gathering without rest. The expositors observe the ten homers the least: a vast heap for every man. The expositors observe the spread about the camp: the flesh laid out in plenty. The expositors observe the insatiable lust: grasping to excess what they craved. The believer is taught to mark the all day and night, the ten homers the least, the spread about the camp, the insatiable lust, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that the people gathered the quails greedily, day and night, ten homers the least — their insatiable grasping betraying the lust that would soon be their bane.
33:
"And while the flesh was yet between their teeth, ere it was chewed, the wrath of the LORD was kindled against the people, and the LORD smote the people with a very great plague." The witnesses note the judgment falling while the flesh was yet in their mouths — a very great plague. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was when they had just got it into their mouths and were about to bite it. The witnesses note that Clarke notes some heavy judgment fell on the murmurers, of what kind the sacred writer says nothing; Ellicott reads "a very great plague," the noun cognate to "smote." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the flesh between their teeth — the judgment falling at the moment of indulgence; the the wrath kindled — god's anger against their lust; the a very great plague — a heavy stroke upon the murmurers; the sin's own judgment — the craving punished in the act (Ps. 106:15).

The witnesses agree the swiftness of the stroke marked the sin's gravity: the judgment fell at the very moment of indulgence, the flesh still between their teeth, that the connection between the lust and its punishment might be unmistakable; God let them taste their craving but no more, smiting them in the act, a fearful sign that sin gratified in defiance of God carries its judgment in its own hand (cf. Ps. 106:15).

So the witnesses make the verse the plague in the act: The witnesses note the the flesh between their teeth: the judgment falling at the moment of indulgence. The witnesses note the the wrath kindled: God's anger against their lust. The witnesses note the a very great plague: a heavy stroke upon the murmurers. The witnesses note the sin's own judgment: the craving punished in the act (Ps. 106:15). The expositors observe the the flesh between their teeth: the judgment falling at the moment of indulgence. The expositors observe the the wrath kindled: God's anger against their lust. The expositors observe the a very great plague: a heavy stroke upon the murmurers. The expositors observe the sin's own judgment: the craving punished in the act (Ps. 106:15). The believer is taught to mark the the flesh between their teeth, the the wrath kindled, the a very great plague, the sin's own judgment, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that the plague fell while the flesh was yet in their mouths — the judgment swift upon the lust, sin gratified in defiance of God carrying its punishment in its own hand (Ps. 106:15).
34:
"And he called the name of that place Kibrothhattaavah: because there they buried the people that lusted." The witnesses note the place named Kibroth-hattaavah, "the graves of lust," where the lusting people were buried. The witnesses note that Gill notes the name signifies "the graves of lust" — graves dug by lust, where those who indulged it were buried. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the name from this judgment, the place of encampment so called. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Kibroth-hattaavah — "the graves of lust."; the dug by lust — their craving its own undoing; the they buried the people — the lusters laid in the ground; the a perpetual epitaph — inordinate desire ending in death.

The witnesses agree the name stands as a perpetual epitaph upon the sin: the very graves were called "the graves of lust," for it was their craving that dug them; the place-name preserved the warning, that they who would not be content with God's provision but lusted after flesh found their flesh become their grave — a solemn monument that inordinate desire, pressed against God, ends in death.

So the witnesses make the verse the graves of lust: The witnesses note the Kibroth-hattaavah: "the graves of lust." The witnesses note the dug by lust: their craving its own undoing. The witnesses note the they buried the people: the lusters laid in the ground. The witnesses note the a perpetual epitaph: inordinate desire ending in death. The expositors observe the Kibroth-hattaavah: "the graves of lust." The expositors observe the dug by lust: their craving its own undoing. The expositors observe the they buried the people: the lusters laid in the ground. The expositors observe the a perpetual epitaph: inordinate desire ending in death. The believer is taught to mark the Kibroth-hattaavah, the dug by lust, the they buried the people, the a perpetual epitaph, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that the place was named "the graves of lust," where the lusting people were buried — a perpetual epitaph that inordinate desire, pressed against God, ends in death.
35:
"And the people journeyed from Kibrothhattaavah unto Hazeroth; and abode at Hazeroth." The witnesses note Israel journeying on from the graves of lust to Hazeroth. The witnesses note that Gill notes they stayed at Kibroth-hattaavah a month or more, then journeyed to Hazeroth and abode there at least seven days (Num. 12:15). The witnesses note that the march continued from the place of judgment. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from Kibroth-hattaavah — leaving the graves of lust behind; the unto Hazeroth — the next station of the march; the abode there — staying at least seven days; the the pilgrimage continued — god leading them on despite their sin.

The witnesses agree the brief verse moves the people on from the scene of their sin: leaving the graves of lust behind, they journeyed to Hazeroth, the pilgrimage continuing despite all their murmuring; God did not cast them off but led them forward still, though their way was now marked with the graves of the disobedient — a sober reminder that the people of God press on toward the land, but leave behind them, at each stage, the monuments of their failures.

So the witnesses make the verse the journey onward: The witnesses note the from Kibroth-hattaavah: leaving the graves of lust behind. The witnesses note the unto Hazeroth: the next station of the march. The witnesses note the abode there: staying at least seven days. The witnesses note the the pilgrimage continued: God leading them on despite their sin. The expositors observe the from Kibroth-hattaavah: leaving the graves of lust behind. The expositors observe the unto Hazeroth: the next station of the march. The expositors observe the abode there: staying at least seven days. The expositors observe the the pilgrimage continued: God leading them on despite their sin. The believer is taught to mark the from Kibroth-hattaavah, the unto Hazeroth, the abode there, the the pilgrimage continued, and to trust the hand of the LORD. The verse thus records that Israel journeyed from the graves of lust to Hazeroth — the pilgrimage continuing, God leading them on, though their way was marked with the monuments of their failures.
